segments.s492-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is 3rd-person past tense 'he heard' and occurs before its subject (poetic verb–subject order). قیدافه is the subject (a proper name). این از پسر is the object phrase: این 'this' (the news/thing just mentioned) qualified by از پسر 'from the son' (i.e., he heard this from/about the son).
segments.s492-g34: دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش). گشت is the past of گشتن used as a copular/changes-of-state verb 'became/was turned'. زان 'from/for that' gives the cause, and درد 'pain' here means emotional sorrow. زیر و زبر is an idiomatic binomial 'under and over' meaning 'upside down/overturned' and describes the state of the heart.
segments.s492-g35: از ایوان is a source/place phrase 'from the iwan/porch'. فرستاده را is the definite object (را marking the messenger). پیش خواند is a compound sense of خواند with the adverb پیش meaning 'before/in the presence of' — together here it means 'called forward/summoned (into his presence)'. Note the verb follows the noun phrase for poetic order.
segments.s492-g36: به is the preposition 'to/onto'. تخت is 'throne' and گرانمایگان is a plural epithet 'the illustrious/exalted ones' (a genitive/epithet attached to تخت). برنشاند is a past causative/causative-perfect verb 'set/placed (him) upon' (بر- prefix + نشاند). The whole phrase means 'seated him on the throne of the illustrious.'
segments.s492-g37: فراوان is an adverb 'much/at length' modifying the verb. بپرسید is past 3rd-person 'he asked/questioned'. و 'and' links the clauses. بنواختش (نواخت + ـش) has the object clitic -ش 'him' and here means 'entertained/played (for) him' (i.e., provided music or entertainment).
segments.s492-g38: یکی here is an indefinite 'one/a certain' (introducing a single person/thing). مایه‌ور is an adjective 'wealthy'. جایگه is 'place/position' (rank or post), and ساختش (ساخت + ـش) is past 'made/appointed him' (causative meaning 'made him [into] that place'). The clause says he assigned him a rich post or place.
segments.s492-g39: فرستاد is past 'he sent/dispatched'. هرگونه‌ای is an indefinite quantifier 'of every kind/every sort'. خوردنی is a noun/adjective 'edible/food' (here functioning as a noun meaning 'food items'). The verb governs the noun phrase and indicates he sent all sorts of food.
segments.s492-g40: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' used to enumerate categories. پوشیدنی 'garments/clothing' is the next category; هم 'also' links it; ز گستردنی lists another category introduced by the same preposition: گستردنی literally 'things to be spread' or 'spreadable' (here items laid out, e.g. coverings or spreadable goods). The phrase enumerates food, clothing, and spread-out articles.
segments.s492-g41: بشد is a past/impersonal verb meaning 'it became/it happened' placed before its adverbial phrase (poetic inversion). آن شب و بامداد پگاه is an adverbial temporal phrase ('that night and the dawn'), with two near-synonymous nouns (بامداد, پگاه) stacked for emphasis.
segments.s492-g42: به پرسش بیامد is a purposive construction: به + noun (پرسش) + آمد (here in the archaic form بیامد) = 'came to ask/with a question'; بیامد is simple past third-person. به درگاه شاه is the destination ('into/unto the king's court/presence'), with the second به marking direction/goal.
segments.s492-g43: پرستندگان (subject, plural) + پرده (direct object) + برداشتند (past, 3rd pl.) is a normal SOV pattern: 'the attendants removed/lifted the curtain.' پرده is the object and there is no explicit object-marker را here (poetic economy).
segments.s492-g44: بر اسپش marks location/placement ('upon his steed') with بر before the possessed noun اسپش (اسپ + enclitic -ش 'his'). ز درگاه is an adverbial 'from the court' (ز = from). بگذاشتند (past, 3rd pl.) is transitive 'they placed/they set' and here implies putting him on the horse and sending him away from the court; the subject is the same پرستندگان from the previous clause.
segments.s492-g45: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when.' قیدافه را is the definite/direct object (name + object marker را). دید is past tense 'saw'; بر تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'on (an) ivory throne' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s492-g46: ز before the gems (ز یاقوت و پیروزه) indicates material/composition or 'of' in poetic diction ('of ruby and turquoise'). بر سرش تاج places the noun تاج ('crown') after its locative phrase 'on his head' (سر + enclitic ش = 'his') — an inverted/poetic word order meaning 'a crown of ruby and turquoise on his head.'
segments.s492-g47: ز + noun phrase here introduces the manner/material: 'in/with gold-brocade' (زربفت). پوشیده is a participle/adjective 'covered/clad,' so ز زربفت پوشیده چینی قبای reads as 'clad in gold-brocade, a Chinese robe' with چینی (modifier) before قبای (robe). The order is compact/poetic: preposition + descriptive participle + noun.
segments.s492-g48: This is a verbless, nominal-phrase description common in poetry: فراوان (numeral/adverb 'many/abundant') + پرستنده (worshippers/devotees) + گردش (a noun meaning 'circling, a circuit') + به پای ('at the feet' where به marks location). Together: 'many worshippers circling at (his) feet.' The verb 'were' or equivalent is omitted but implied.
segments.s492-g73: به + infinitive here introduces the concurrent action ('as/while'); می‌خوردن is the present/stative form 'was eating' used narratively to mark ongoing action. اندر governs the following noun phrase and means 'in/among'; گرانمایه is an adjective 'precious/costly' modifying the following noun شاه 'the king' (possessive sense: the king's costly things or the costly setting of the king). Word order is poetic: temporal clause (به می‌خوردن) + location (اندر گرانمایه شاه).
segments.s492-g74: فزون کرد is a two-part verbal idea: فزون (more/increasing) + کرد (past of 'to do/make') = 'made more' / 'increased' and functions here as 'turned more (his gaze)'. سوی + proper name indicates direction 'toward Alexander'; نگاه is the object 'gaze / glance'. The sequence places the degree-word before کرد (poetic order) and the phrase as a whole means 'he looked increasingly toward Alexander.'
segments.s492-g75: به marks the indirect object 'to'; گنجور here refers to the treasury/treasurer (the person in charge of the treasure). گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' (past). آن + adjective + noun (درخشان حریر) = 'that shining silk' (آن = demonstrative 'that', درخشان modifies حریر). The whole segment is the speech-introducing clause 'he said to the treasurer (about) that shining silk.'
segments.s492-g76: نوشته is a noun/verbal noun 'writing' used here like an imperative passive ('let it be written' / 'have it written'); برو functions as a light verb/particle indicating placement or direction onto the silk (classical/poetic usage: 'write upon'). صورت دلپذیر is the manner or form of the inscription: صورت 'form/appearance' modified by دلپذیر 'pleasing'. Together: 'inscribe upon it in a pleasing form.'
segments.s492-g77: به پیش من آور is an imperative clause: به + پیش + من = 'before me / into my presence' and آور is the imperative 'bring'. چنان هم که هست is a comparative/relative clause 'just as it is' — چنان 'thus/so', هم used here as emphatic 'just', که هست relative/correlative 'that which is / as it is', هست the copula 'is'.
segments.s492-g78: به تندی برو is a sequence of adverb + imperative: به تندی 'with haste' and برو 'go!' — an instruction to act quickly. هیچ مبسای دست is an admonition not to hold back: هیچ 'not at all/none' + مبسای (an archaic/poetic negative admonitory form meaning 'spare/withhold' — here used in the sense 'do not spare/do not hesitate') + دست 'hand' (metonymy for effort/action). Together: 'go quickly; do not hold back any hand/effort.'
segments.s492-g79: بیاورد is past 'brought/produced'; گنجور here is the agent 'the treasurer' (poetic diction allows verb before its agent). و 'and' links the next verb; بنهاد is past of نهادن 'placed/laid' meaning 'he placed (it)'; پیش 'before/in front of' indicates the location of placement (before the king). The clause reports the treasurer bringing and placing the object before the king.
segments.s492-g80: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; دیدش is the past of 'see' with enclitic -ش 'it' = 'he saw it'; نگه کرد is 'he made/held a gaze' (نگه 'gaze' + کرد auxiliary = 'fixed his look'); ز اندازه بیش is a prepositional expression 'beyond measure' (ز = 'from/because of' here used poetically to mark excess, اندازه = measure/degree, بیش = more/excess). Altogether: 'when he saw it, he stared beyond measure.'
segments.s492-g65: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he ordered/said' (literary). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'that/so that'. خوان here means 'banquet/table (for eating)' and is the object of the subordinate clause. بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they arranged/ornamented' (بی- + arastan in plural), completing the subordinate purpose clause introduced by تا.
segments.s492-g66: پرستنده is a noun 'admirers/devotees' (agent noun). رود here is the musical 'rūd' (string instrument) used in poetic contexts, so the phrase means the admirers played the lute. و is the coordinating 'and'. می‌خواستند is imperfect/past habitual plural 'they wished/desired' and connects to what those admirers wanted to do next.
segments.s492-g67: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they placed/established' (verb). یک خانه is 'a house/room' and خوانهای is a genitive/attributive use of خوان 'banquet/feasting-hall' (here 'a house for the feast'). ساج specifies the material (saj wood) as an attributive noun. Word order is verb — object — modifiers in this literary line.
segments.s492-g68: همه 'all/entirely' modifies پیکرش 'its body/structure' (possessive -ش). زر is 'gold' describing the material of the body. و is the conjunction 'and'. کوکبش 'its koakb' uses کوکب metaphorically for ornamental bosses/points and عاج 'ivory' names their material; the phrase lists materials of different parts.
segments.s492-g69: خورشهای is the plural (or collective) of خورش 'stew/dish' and is the subject/thing being reported. بسیار 'many' quantifies it. آورده شد is a passive/perfect construction 'were brought' (آورده + شد), describing that many dishes arrived.
segments.s492-g70: می‌آورد is an imperfect/progressive 'they were bringing' or 'more were brought' (habitual/continuous action). و is 'and'. چون here functions as a temporal 'when' in the poetic register (not listed among the given senses), introducing the subordinate clause. خوردنی is a noun/adjective 'food/edible things', خورده شد is passive perfect 'had been eaten/was eaten', so the clause means 'and when the food had been eaten...'.
segments.s492-g71: طبقهای is the plural construct 'plates/trays'. زرین 'golden' modifies طبقها; و is 'and'. نهاد here is the past singular/plural form of نهادن used intransitively/actively 'placed/set' (in poetry object/subject inversion is common), indicating the act of setting the plates.
segments.s492-g72: نخستین 'first' sets a temporal/order marker. ز is the poetic form of 'from/of' ('about' or 'concerning') and introduces قیدافه, here treated as a proper name or special term. کردند is the past plural 'they made/did', and یاد 'remembrance/mention' is the object: 'first they made mention of Qeydafeh.'
segments.s492-g17: پذیره شدش is a passive/perfect-style construction: پذیره (reception/being welcomed) + شد (became/was) with the clitic ش marking the third‑person object ('him'). با introduces the accompaniment 'with', and سپاهی گران is a noun + adjective ('army' + 'heavy/mighty') functioning as the instrument/party by which he was received.
segments.s492-g18: همه is a quantifier 'all/every'; it scopes over the coordinated nouns نامداران و نیک اختران. نامداران is a plural agentive 'the famed/renowned ones'. نیک اختران is a poetic compound: نیک 'good/auspicious' modifying اختران ('stars' used metaphorically for noble/chosen persons).
segments.s492-g19: This clause orders subject and modifiers before the verb: پسر 'the son' + نیز 'also'; چون here functions as a temporal/concessive connector 'when/as' linking to the verb. مادرش را is the definite possessive object 'his mother' (مادر + possessive ش + object marker را). The verb بدید is past 'saw.'
segments.s492-g20: پیاده شد is a light‑verb expression meaning 'became on foot' → 'dismounted/got down.' The conjunction و links it to the following verb phrase آفرین گسترید, where آفرین is an exclamation/noun 'praise/bravo' and گسترید (past) 'spread/uttered' here means 'uttered/poured forth praise.'
segments.s492-g21: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said.' The proper name قیدافه follows as its subject (classical word order allows verb before subject). تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result 'so that/until,' and برنشست is the past tense 'she sat (upon it / took her seat).' Together it expresses an order resulting in her sitting.
segments.s492-g22: همی is an enclitic adverb conveying continuity/ongoing action ('still/kept on'); راند is the past form 'he rode/drove on.' The participial phrase دستش گرفته به دست is adjectival/circumstantial: دستش 'her hand' (with possessive ش), گرفته 'having taken' (perfect participle), and به دست 'into (his) hand' — i.e. he took and held her hand in his own.
segments.s492-g23: بدو is the prepositional form 'to him' (classical 'به + او'). قیدروش here is a proper name (the given senses list treats it as a common noun, so the lexical senses don't fit). آنچ is an archaic relative 'that which/what' and takes the verbs دید و شنید 'saw and heard' in the past tense; the two verbs form a compound verbal predicate 'saw and heard that/those things.'
segments.s492-g24: همی گفت is a continuous past 'he kept saying' (همی marking ongoing action + گفت past of گفتن). و links to the result clause رنگ رخش ناپدید: رنگ 'color/complexion' + رخش (رخ + ش) — here 'رخ' means face/countenance and ش is the possessive clitic 'his' — and ناپدید 'vanished/was gone.'
segments.s492-g145: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is a prepositional dativelike clitic/phrase 'to him' placed before the verb گفت (past 'said'). کای is a vocative interjection 'O', and مهتر پرخرد is the vocative title phrase 'chief/elder of great wisdom' addressed to the addressee.
segments.s492-g146: چنین گفتن = a verbal-noun phrase 'such saying / to say such a thing'. از تو = 'from you' (marks the source/agent of the spoken remark). نه اندر خورد is a negative predicate: نه (not) + اندر خورد (archaic/poetic verb 'to fit / to befit / to be appropriate'), so the clause means 'is not fitting.'
segments.s492-g147: منم = 'I am' (the copular/emphatic form with enclitic -م). بیطقون is a proper name (here the speaker's name). کدخدای جهان = 'kadeh-khoda (village-head) of the world' — a grandiose title; word order is noun + epithet.
segments.s492-g148: چنین تخمه فیلقوسم = 'such a seed/offspring (takhmeh) as my Filqus' — فیلقوسم likely contains the proper name فیلقوس plus the possessive suffix -م ('my' or 'of me'). مخوان is a negative imperative (مِخوان) meaning 'do not call/recite/name'; here it forbids naming the speaker's line in that way. Overall: 'Do not call my clan Filqus' or 'do not name me such a scion.'
segments.s492-g149: سپاسم ز یزدان پروردگار = 'I give thanks to Yezdan (God) the Lord.' سپاسم functions as an expression 'my thanks' / 'I thank' followed by ز (from/to) marking the beneficiary/recipient (God) and the epithet پروردگار ('the Lord').
segments.s492-g150: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / for'. با من نبد مهتری نامدار = 'with me there was not (نبد) a famed (نامدار) superior/preeminence (مهتری)'; نبد is an archaic negative existential 'was not' so the clause expresses gratitude that no renowned superior existed over him.
segments.s492-g151: که بردی به شاه جهان آگهی = 'that/for you carried news to the king of the world.' بردی is 2nd-person past 'you carried/took'; به + شاه جهان marks the goal 'to the king of the world'; آگهی (information/news) is the object placed after the goal.
segments.s492-g152: تنم را = 'my body' (تن + possessive -م) with the object marker را. ز جان = 'of life' (literally 'from life') and زود + کردی + تهی = adverb 'quickly' (زود) + past 2nd-person 'you did' (کردی) + predicate adjective 'empty' (تهی): idiomatically 'you quickly made my body empty of life' = 'you quickly deprived me of life.' Word order places the adverb before the verb; را marks the direct object.
segments.s492-g89: که introduces an indirect command ('tell ... that ...'). قیدافه is a proper name (the addressee) modified by پاکدل 'pure‑hearted' which follows the name as an epithet; را is the definite/object marker attached to the whole noun phrase. بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'say/tell' (here used to issue the command 'tell X ...').
segments.s492-g90: که here links the reported command to its content ('that ...'). جز is the preposition 'except' limiting راستی 'truth'. در زمانه is a locative phrase 'in the age / in the world' and مجوی is a prohibitive 2nd‑person form 'do not seek' (classical/poetic imperative), so together: 'seek nothing in the world except truth.'
segments.s492-g91: نگر is an imperative 'look / see / mark well' used to call attention. سر 'head' is the noun governed by the verb نپیچی 'do not turn' (negative imperative/subjunctive: نـ + پیچی), and ز + فرمان من means 'from my command' (ز = from, فرمان = command, من = my). The clause means 'do not turn your head away from my command.'
segments.s492-g92: نگه‌دار here functions as an instruction 'be a keeper/guard' or 'keep/guard' (imperative sense), بیدار is an adjective/adverb 'awake/alert' describing the manner, and پیمان من means 'my covenant/pledge'. Together: 'be an alert guardian of my covenant' / 'keep my pledge well and be watchful.'
segments.s492-g93: وگر is the conditional 'if'. هیچ تاب here means 'any strength/endurance' (هیچ used in an indefinite/conditional sense), اندر = 'in/within', and آری is an archaic/formal present meaning 'you have' or 'there is in you' (here used of capacity in the heart). به دل locates that capacity 'in your heart.'
segments.s492-g94: بیارم is a 1st‑person verb 'I will bring' / 'I shall bring' (here announcing the speaker's action). یکی 'one / a single', لشکری 'a force / troop' (nominal), and دل گسل is a compound epithet literally 'heart‑splitting' (a force that breaks hearts) modifying the troop. The line means 'I will bring one troop, a heart‑breaker.'
segments.s492-g95: نشان is 'sign / token', هنرهای تو is the plural noun 'your arts/skills' (هنر + plural -ها with genitive تو 'your'), and یافتم is the 1st‑person past 'I found / I perceived'. The clause states that the speaker has observed evidence of the addressee's skill.
segments.s492-g96: به جنگ آمدن is an infinitival/nominal phrase 'to come to battle / to go to war'; تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swiftly / keen', and نشتافتم is the past 1st‑person of شتافتن 'I hastened / I rushed'. Together they mean 'I hastened swiftly to go to war.'
segments.s492-g121: زمینش is ground/ground-floor (زمین) with the possessive suffix -ش ‘his’. همه modifies the following materials, meaning the whole/entire surface is made of them. صندل و چوب عود is a coordinated noun phrase: صندل = sandalwood and چوب عود = ‘wood of ʿūd’ (agarwood); word order places the general word (همه) before the list of materials.
segments.s492-g122: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. The parallel ز ... و ز ... lists the materials (جزع and پیروزه). او را is a dative/beneficiary + object-marking sequence meaning 'for him' (او = him/her; را marks definiteness/emphasis). عمود is a noun 'pillar/column'; the verb ('they made/placed') is omitted — a common poetic ellipsis — so the phrase reads 'from jasper and turquoise, (they) a pillar for him'.
segments.s492-g123: سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander'. فروماند is an older/past form meaning 'was overcome/worn out/abashed' (stative/result), so the clause says Alexander was humbled/overcome. زان = 'from that' / 'because of that', and جایگاه is 'place/position' — the prepositional phrase locates the cause or place of his abasement.
segments.s492-g124: ازان again means 'from that' or 'because of that', introducing what is affected. فر = royal splendour/glory, اورنگ = throne, and آن دستگاه = 'that court/establishment' — a list of grand things tied to the kingly position. The verb is again omitted (poetic ellipsis): these things are implicitly affected by what precedes.
segments.s492-g125: همی here is the iterative/continuous adverb 'kept/was continually'. گفت is the verb 'said' (note: the supplied lemma senses listed 'conversation' rather than the verb; here it functions as the past tense verb 'he said' or 'he kept saying'). کاینت (کاین + ت) = 'this/that (which)'; سرای نشست is a compound meaning the 'hall/assembly-place' (سرای here = house/hall; نشست = a sitting/assembly). The whole segment introduces what he keeps saying about that assembly-place.
segments.s492-g126: نبیند is the negative form of the verb 'بیند/دیدن' (to see); in narrative it functions as 'has not seen / never saw' or 'did not see'. چنین = 'such/like this', جای = 'place', and یزدان‌پرست = 'God-worshipper' (here adjectival/noun for the pious community). The clause reports his exclamation that he had never seen such a pious gathering/place.
segments.s492-g127: خرامان is an adverb/gerund meaning 'with a stately/proud gait' and modifies the verb. بیامد is the simple past 'he came' (literally 'came forward'), and به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' indicating movement toward the king.
segments.s492-g128: نهادند is the past plural verb 'they put/placed/set'. زرین is the adjective 'golden'. یکی is used in the sense 'one / a certain', introducing a single item, and زیرگاه is a noun 'place beneath' / 'under-seat' (i.e., a footstool or low seat). Together: 'they placed a golden footstool (for him)'; the beneficiary is understood from context.
segments.s492-g49: A noun phrase plus a simile. رخ شاه تابان = 'the king's shining face' with the possessive relation implied (رخ [face] + شاه [king]) and تابان postpositive as an adjective 'shining/illustrious'. به کردار هور is a simile: به + کردار = 'in the manner/conduct' and هور = 'splendor/sun' (i.e. 'like the sun').
segments.s492-g50: A verbless predication typical of classical Persian: نشستن here is a noun/infinitive 'the sitting/seat', گهش is 'گه' with the enclitic -ش 'his' (i.e. 'his place/occasion'), and را is the definite/accusative marker attaching to that noun phrase. ستونها بلور is the predicate with the copula omitted: 'the pillars were crystal' → 'the crystal pillars were his sitting-place'.
segments.s492-g51: A noun phrase describing a covering. زبر qualifies پوششی as an outer/upper covering (زبر = 'upper, above'); جزع بسته is a participial phrase in which بسته ('bound/fastened') modifies جزع (here acting as a noun phrase element), and به زر indicates the material/instrument 'with gold'. The clause describes how the covering is fastened or trimmed with gold.
segments.s492-g52: Verbal/adjectival phrase: بافته is a past participle 'woven/embroidered', governing the following object دانه‌های گهر ('strings/beads of jewel'). برو here functions adverbially/locatively in the poetic text (literally 'on/over it' or as a prefixed element indicating 'overlaid'), so the whole means 'overlaid/woven with jewel-beads.'
segments.s492-g53: A noun with accompanying instruments: پرستنده 'admirer/devotee' or 'one who adores', followed by two prepositional phrases introduced by با 'with' (repeated for emphasis) listing ornaments (طوق 'necklace/collar', گوشوار 'earring/ornament'). The repetition of با is emphatic/coordining in poetic diction.
segments.s492-g54: A locative phrase giving the position: به پای اندر = 'at the foot/in front of' (به + پای + اندر), آن گلشن = 'that garden' and زرنگار is a postpositive adjective 'gold-decorated/gilded'. The whole means 'at the foot within that gilded flower-garden.'
segments.s492-g55: Simple verbal clause. سکندر is the subject. ب + دان together form the classical compound بدان = 'at that' (the prepositional phrase 'at that/thereupon'); درشگفتی is an adverbial phrase 'in astonishment', and بماند is the verbal form 'he remained' (classical narrative form with the prefixal/preterite sense).
segments.s492-g56: Adverbial and verbal clause: فراوان intensifies or indicates 'many times/abundantly', نهان 'secretly', نام یزدان 'the name of God' (construct of name + divine name), and بخواند is the narrative past/third-person verb 'he uttered/recited/called' (here 'invoked'). The order is typical of epic Persian with adverbs preceding the verb.
segments.s492-g169: This phrase has the normal classical Persian word order Subject (سکندر) – indirect object (بدو) – verb (گفت). سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'; بدو is the prepositional/indirect-object form 'to him'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'. The following relative expression هر کز begins a subordinate clause ('whoever...').
segments.s492-g170: This continues the relative clause started by هر کز. به here functions as a locative/instrumental sense 'in/regarding' (’in manhood’), مردی is used abstractly as 'manliness' or 'noble manhood', بود is the past copula 'was', خواستار is an adjective 'desirous', and جهان is the object 'the world'. The clause reads roughly 'whoever among the great was noble in manhood and desirous of the world.'
segments.s492-g171: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / should not'; که introduces the following subordinate clause. پیچد is a present-subjunctive/optative-form meaning 'turn/veer' (here with prohibitive scope: 'must not turn'), ز is the poetic preposition 'from', راه 'path', and گزند 'harm/damage' (here 'the path of safety/escape' or 'the path free from harm').
segments.s492-g172: The second که introduces the purpose/result clause ('lest/so that ... not'). بد دل is a compound meaning 'ill‑hearted / evil‑minded' (بد modifies دل), به + گیتی = 'in the world', نگردد is a negative present/subjunctive of گردیدن 'to become/turn' (here 'become'), and بلند is an adjective 'high/exalted'. Together: 'lest an evil-hearted person become exalted in the world.'
segments.s492-g173: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. با here means 'with' (indicating possession/association). منستی is a poetic/archaic possessive/nominal form implying 'if it were with you / if you had (it)' (the form is elliptical/poetic), سلیحم is 'my weapon' (sword), and کنون means 'now'. The whole clause reads 'If now my weapon were with you.'
segments.s492-g174: همه خانه = 'every house' (همه before a noun is distributive 'all/every'); گشتی is a past/potential/resultative verbal form meaning 'you would make / you would cause to become' (poetic conjugation; the exact morphological details are archaic), چو = 'like/as', دریای = 'sea of', خون = 'blood' (here 'a sea of blood' = widespread slaughter).
segments.s492-g175: This clause uses an object-marked pronoun and compacted verb forms. ت + را is the object 'you' (written here as two pieces: second-person marker + object marker). کشتمی‌گر is an archaic/poetic verbal or agentive form related to 'kill' (the precise literary form is irregular/obsolete here), جگرگاه means the inner breast/heart area ('innermost'/'bosom'), and خوی + ش together form 'his/one's own' (خوی = root meaning 'disposition/kin' but here part of the possessive unit; ش = third-person possessive). The phrase expresses 'I would slay you, [even] at the cost of my own breast/heart.'
segments.s492-g176: بدریدمی is an irregular/obscure poetic form meaning roughly 'tearing (myself)'; پیش = 'before / in the presence of', بدخواه = 'enemy / ill-wisher', and خوی + ش again marks 'one's own' (here 'my enemy' or 'before my foe' depending on syntax). Together the segment gives a vivid image: 'tearing my own breast before my foe.' These forms are highly compressed and archaic, common in epic diction.
segments.s492-g81: بدانست is a past-tense verb (VSO order here) meaning “knew/realized”; the subject قیدافه follows the verb (typical classical/poetic order). کو introduces the subordinate interrogative/relative clause “where …” and قیصرست contains the noun قیصر plus the bound copula -ست (“is/was”), so the clause means “where Caesar was.”
segments.s492-g82: بران is an adverbial/relational particle meaning “over/on/with respect to that,” attaching to لشکر “army/host”; نامور is an adjective “renowned” modifying مهتر, and مهترست is the noun مهتر plus the copula -ست (‘is’), so the phrase reads “he is the renowned chief/commander over that army.”
segments.s492-g83: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite/agent noun “an envoy/a messenger”; کرده is a past participle/verb “made/sent” that attributes the sending (i.e. “who had sent” or “had sent”); از خویشتن is a prepositional phrase “from/of his own” with خویشتن emphatic “himself/his own,” so the whole unit means “an envoy that he had sent from his own (men)/of his own.”
segments.s492-g84: دلیر is an adjective “brave” describing the envoy; آمدست is a classical/perfective past form meaning “has come/arrived” (verb agrees with the envoy); اندرین is a demonstrative-locative “in this” and انجمن is the noun “assembly/gathering,” so together “a brave man arrived in this assembly.”
segments.s492-g85: بدو is the prepositional phrase “to him/unto him” (i.e. 'he said to him'); گفت is the past verb “said”; کای is the vocative particle “O”; مرد گسترده کام is a vocative noun phrase where مرد is “man” and گسترده کام is a compound/adjunct “of wide/expansive desire,” so the speaker addresses the envoy as “O man of wide desire.”
segments.s492-g86: بگو is a 2nd‑person imperative “say/tell”; تا here introduces the content clause (classical marker roughly “that/what” or “so that”) and links بگو to the reported content; سکندر is the proper name Alexander, چه is the interrogative “what,” دادت is the past verb plus enclitic object -ت “gave you” (i.e. “he gave you”), and پیام is the noun “message.” The clause therefore asks “tell what message Alexander gave you.”
segments.s492-g87: چنین is an adverb “thus/so”; داد is the past verb “gave” and with پاسخ (“answer”) forms the expression “gave such an answer” (چنین داد پاسخ = ‘he replied thus’); که introduces the reported clause that follows. شاه جهان is the compound subject “king of the world” (an honorific/title).
segments.s492-g88: سخن گفت is a verbal phrase “spoke/said” (سخن = speech, گفت = spoke); با من is the prepositional object “with me”; میان مهان is a locative phrase “among the guests” (میان = among, مهان = guests/visitors). Together they report the content: “the king of the world spoke with me among the guests.”
segments.s492-g1: This is a simple past action: the subject جهانجوی (an epithet, 'world-seeker') comes first, the object ده نامور ('ten famed ones') follows, and برگزید is the past tense verb 'chose/selected' agreeing with the singular subject. جهانجوی is an archaic/poetic epithet and ده نامور is a numerical + adjective phrase functioning as the direct object.
segments.s492-g2: ز مردان رومی is a prepositional phrase 'from the Roman men / from among the Rûm', and چنانچون سزید is a dependent manner clause 'as was fitting' where چنانچون introduces the manner and سزید is the past 3rd-person singular of سزایدن ('to be fitting/deserve'). Together the clause qualifies how he selected them.
segments.s492-g3: که introduces a relative/clausal link ('who/that'); بودند is the past plural copula 'they were'; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely'; هم‌آواز qualifies them as 'of one accord/in unison' and اوی is the third-person pronoun 'with him' (here marking the person they are in accord with). The whole clause means 'who were wholly like-minded with him.'
segments.s492-g4: نگه داشتندی is a past plural compound verbal idea: نگه (keeping/watch) + داشتندی (archaic past plural of داشتن 'to keep') = 'they kept/maintained'; همه راز اوی is the object 'all his secrets' with اوی as the possessive 'his'. Poetic word order omits an object marker (را) but meaning is direct object: 'they kept all his secrets.'
segments.s492-g5: چنین here is an adverb 'thus/so' and functions with گفت (the reporting verb 'said'); کاکنون is a temporal adverb 'now'; به راه اندرون is a directional prepositional phrase 'into/toward the inner path/way'. Word order places the report + temporal phrase before the quoted injunction that follows. (Note: the provided sense list for گفت did not include the verbal sense 'said', so that specific sense is marked as unavailable.)
segments.s492-g6: مخوانید here functions as a negative prohibitive imperative/present addressed to plural addressees—classical Persian often uses the prefix م- to form prohibitions ('do not ...'); its sense here is 'do not call/summon'. ما را is the object 'us' (را is the definite object marker), and جز از بیقطون is an exception phrase 'except by/from Biqṭūn' (بیقطون is a proper name/epithet). The clause reads 'do not summon us into the inner way except by Biqṭūn.'
segments.s492-g7: همی رفت is an epic/classical imperfective-past construction meaning 'he kept going' or 'he proceeded' (همی marks continuous aspect). پیش اندرون is a directional phrase 'before/toward the inner [quarters]'. قیدروش here functions adverbially/qualifying manner 'in his custom/way' (i.e. 'in his usual manner'); together: 'he proceeded toward the inner quarters in his customary way.'
segments.s492-g8: سکندر is the proper subject 'Alexander'; سپرده is the past (or perfect) form 'entrusted' (here a verb meaning 'he entrusted'); بدو is the clitic/phrase 'to him'; چشم و گوش are literal 'eye and ear(s)' used idiomatically as objects meaning 'sight and hearing' or 'watchers' (i.e. 'Alexander entrusted to him eyes and ears' = he made him his eyes and ears).
segments.s492-g57: نشستن here is a verbal noun/gerund 'sitting' placed before the verb (classical inverted order). گهی is an adverb 'sometimes'; دید is the past tense 'saw'. مهتر is the person seen, 'the elder/chief'. که نیز functions as a connective ('and/that also') linking this clause to the following clause.
segments.s492-g58: This clause expresses an idiom: 'نیامد ... به چیز' = 'was of no consequence / did not amount to anything'. نیامد is literal past 'did not come' but here functions idiomatically; ورا is the dat/acc pronoun 'to/for him' (i.e. 'for him it did not matter'); روم و ایران are the two nouns 'Rome and Iran'; به چیز is the phrase 'to/for a thing' creating the idiom 'not worth anything'.
segments.s492-g59: بر مهتر آمد is a prepositional phrase + past verb meaning 'approached/went up to the elder' (بر + noun + آمد). زمین داد بوس is a literal sequence 'gave the earth a kiss' (داد + بوس), an idiom for prostrating/kissing the ground; داد is the past 'gave' and بوس here denotes the kiss (verb sense).
segments.s492-g60: چنانچون is a comparative/adverb 'just as/just so'; بود is the past copula 'were'; مردم چاپلوس is the nominal predicate 'the people [were] flatterers' (چاپلوس as predicate adjective/noun). The clause gives the reason/character of the crowd.
segments.s492-g61: ورا دید is 'he/they saw him' — ورا is the classical object pronoun ('to/for him' functioning as 'him') preceding the verb دید. قیدافه is a proper name (the performer or person). بنواختش is a past verb with enclitic -ش ('-him' or '-it') from بنواختن, here 'struck/played (an instrument) for him' or 'struck him'; the verb carries the object clitic referring back to ورا or another animate object.
segments.s492-g62: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (questioned), with بسیار as an adverb 'much/at length'. و simply joins the next action. بنشاختش is a past verb with enclitic (بنشاخت + -ش) meaning 'set him (down)/seated him' or 'placed him', i.e. after questioning they caused him to sit.
segments.s492-g63: چو ... بگشت is a temporal clause 'when ... turned/passed'. چو = 'when'; خورشید تابان = 'the shining sun' (noun + adjective). ز گنبد = 'from the dome' (ز = from). بگشت is the past verb 'turned/returned/passed' describing the sun's movement (classical poetic usage).
segments.s492-g64: گه بار is an adverbial phrase 'on one occasion / at one time' (گه = occasion, بار = time/occasion). بیگانه is the subject 'a stranger' and اندر گذشت is the verb phrase 'passed by / went within' (اندر = in/through, گذشت = passed — past).
segments.s492-g9: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آتش here is used adverbially as a simile 'like fire' modifying the verb. همی راند is a classical/poetic imperfective/past habitual construction ('was driving' / 'drove'), with همی marking continuous or repeated action; راند is the verb 'drive'. مهتر ستور is a compound where مهتر ('chief, master') qualifies ستور ('steeds/horses'); together they mean 'the master of the horses' (the horse-master) and serve as the agent of راند. Word order is literary: temporal (چو) + simile (آتش) + verb phrase (همی راند) + agent (مهتر ستور).
segments.s492-g10: به کوهی رسیدند is a straightforward past plural verb phrase: رسیدند ('they arrived/reached') with the preposition به showing motion towards a place ('to a mountain'). سنگش بلور is a verbless nominal clause common in Persian poetry: سنگش ('its stone' — سنگ + enclitic possessive -ش) followed by بلور ('crystal'), i.e. 'its stone was crystal' with the copula understood rather than stated.
segments.s492-g11: بدودر is a past plural verb 'they rushed / hastened' (classical poetic diction). ز here functions in the idiom ز بهر meaning 'toward/for the sake of' and introduces the object/goal; هرگونه‌ای modifies the implied thing sought ('of every kind'). میوه‌دار is an adjective 'fruit-bearing' describing the (elliptical) plants/trees that attract them — in poetry the noun 'trees' or 'plants' is often omitted and supplied by the next segment.
segments.s492-g12: فراوان گیا بود is a copular clause: فراوان ('abundant') modifies گیا ('plants/vegetation'); بود is the past copula 'there was / grew'. بر کوهسار is a locative phrase 'on the mountainside' (بر + کوهسار). Together: 'abundant plants/vegetation were on the mountainside.'
segments.s492-g13: برفتند is the past plural 'they went / proceeded'. زانگونه ('in that manner' / 'thus') modifies the manner of going. پویان is a participial/adjectival plural ('moving, active') describing those who go (it can be an epithet for the travelers). به راه is the directional phrase 'on the road / along the way'. Overall: 'They proceeded onward in that way.'
segments.s492-g14: برآن بوم is a locative phrase: برآن ('on that') + بوم ('land, homeland'), 'on that land'. و is the conjunction 'and'. بر کاندرو repeats the locative particle 'on' plus کاندرو, here functioning as a place-name or locality; classical Persian often uses toponyms of uncertain literal meaning (the dictionary senses list offers figurative meanings). بود شاه is the past existential/copular construction 'there was a king' (بود = 'was', شاه = 'king').
segments.s492-g15: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. قیدافه is a proper name (here the person who notices). آگه شد is the compound past 'became aware / learned' (آگه = aware; شد = became). از introduces the source/content of the awareness ('of / about'), and قیدروش is another personal name or noun referenced — in this line it is the object of 'became aware of'. The whole clause means 'When Qeydafeh became informed about Qeydroush.'
segments.s492-g16: ز بهر is the fixed phrase 'for the sake of / on account of'. پسر is 'son' and پهن is an adjective 'broad' (likely describing the son). بگشاد is a past or aoristic/perfective verbal form 'he opened' (here used with گوش). گوش can mean literal 'ear' or 'attention; listening' — in idiomatic Persian 'گوش بگشاد' = 'he lent an ear / he listened'. So the clause says he gave ear (paid attention) for the broad son.
segments.s492-g113: سکندر is the subject; بیامد is simple past (he came). بران is a poetic contraction of بر آن (“to/on that”) and with بارگاه (palace) forms a prepositional phrase meaning “to that palace.” The verb precedes the locative phrase (a common poetic order) so the clause reads “Alexander came to that palace.”
segments.s492-g114: This is a verbless (nominal) descriptive clause typical in poetry. دو لب = “two lips” (i.e., his two lips); پر ز خنده = the adjective پر (“full”) followed by the poetic preposition ز (“of”) yields “full of laughter.” دل از غم تباه = “heart ruined by sorrow,” where از introduces the cause/agent (“because of/by”) and تباه is an adjectival/ predicative adjective “ruined.”
segments.s492-g115: فرستاده را is the definite direct object (را marks it). دید is the past transitive verb “saw.” سالار بار is a postverbal subject phrase; سالار (chief/commander) + بار (here a noun meaning ‘load/baggage’ or the office responsible for loads) functions as a title, so the sequence is object–verb–subject (typical poetic inversion): “The chief of the baggage saw the messenger.”
segments.s492-g116: بپرسید is past tense ‘he asked/ questioned.’ و links the two actions. بردش here is the past of بردن ‘to take/lead’ with the enclitic object -ش ‘him’ (note: the supplied sense list did not include this verbal meaning, so the appropriate sense is not present). بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase “to/ before the king” where بر = toward/to and شهریار is ‘king.’
segments.s492-g117: همه کاخ او = “his whole/entire palace” (همه modifies کاخ and او is the possessor). پر ز بیگانه بود is the predicate: پر (“full”) + ز (“of”) + بیگانه (“strangers/foreigners”) + بود (past “was”), literally “was full of strangers.” Word order places the possessor and noun before the predicate, a normal descriptive construction.
segments.s492-g118: نشستن here is a deverbal noun meaning a place for sitting / a sitting–room (a common poetic usage), بلورین is the adjective “crystalline” modifying it, and یکی خانه بود = “there was one house/room.” The copula بود closes the nominal statement: “There was one crystal sitting‑chamber.”
segments.s492-g119: This is a list of decorative materials: عقیق (agate) و زبرجد (and peridot). بروبر is an adverb/phrase meaning “face to face / opposite one another,” modifying how the gems are set. نگار here functions as a noun meaning an ornament/decoration (poetic usage derived from ‘painting/adorner’), so the phrase describes agate and peridot arranged opposite each other as decoration.
segments.s492-g120: میان = “among / in the middle of”; اندرون = “inner quarters/within”; گوهر = “gem/jewel”; شاهوار is an adjective meaning “regal / lofty” modifying گوهر. Word order is preposition + place + head noun + adjective: “amid the inner rooms, a regal jewel.”
segments.s492-g137: The verb بخندید (past tense) comes first, a common poetic inversion meaning “laughed.” قیدافه is the subject following the verb. از introduces the cause or reason (’because of / at’), کار here means ‘deed/affair,’ and اوی is a third-person possessive/pronominal form referring back to ‘him’ (’his’); literal order: 'laughed Qeydafeh because-of his deed.'
segments.s492-g138: دلش (دل + ش) is a possessed noun ‘his heart.’ گشت is the past tense of گشتن used as a copula meaning ‘became.’ خرم is a predicative adjective ‘joyful, glad.’ به بازار indicates the place/target (‘to/at the market’), and the final اوی again refers to the same person (possessive/emphatic 'his'); the clause reads 'his heart became joyful at his market.'
segments.s492-g139: ازان پس is a temporal phrase ‘after that/thereupon.’ بدر is here presented as a nominal element immediately followed by کرد (made/did); in classical texts بدر can be a proper name or a lexical item, so the clause reads ‘thereafter Badr did / thereupon he caused.’ کسهای خویش is a possessive phrase ‘his people/folk’ (کسهای = people/folks, خوی + ش = his/own).
segments.s492-g140: فرستاده را is the definite direct object marker construction: ‘the messenger (as object).’ تنگ is an adverb/adjective here meaning ‘close/tightly,’ and بنشاند is an archaic/specialized past form of نشاندن (to seat or set someone); together تنگ بنشاند means ‘had (him) seated close’ or ‘set (him) in a close place.’ پیش at the end means ‘before/in the presence of (him).’ The whole clause: ‘(he) had the messenger seated close before him.’
segments.s492-g141: بدو گفت is a two-word sequence meaning ‘he said to him’ (بدو = ‘to him/unto him,’ گفت = ‘said’). کای is a vocative particle ‘O’ here addressing the addressee, زاده means ‘born/offspring (son of),’ and فیلقوس is a personal name; together: ‘He said to him, “O son of Filqos…”’
segments.s492-g142: همت is the subject meaning ‘ambition/resolve’ or ‘spirit.’ The clause uses a verbless or contracted-predicate style common in poetry: همت بزم و رزمست = ‘(his) spirit/ambition is for both banquet (بزم) and battle (رزم)’, where رزمست is a fused adjectival/predicative form ‘is warlike/pertains to battle.’ و هم نعم و بوس adds a parallel list: ‘and likewise (is) for pleasures/blessings (نعم) and kisses (بوس).’
segments.s492-g143: سکندر is the subject (Alexander). ز here is the poetic/prepositional form meaning ‘from/because of,’ گفتار او is ‘his speech/words.’ گشت is the past copula ‘became/turned,’ and زرد is ‘pale/wan’ — so: ‘Alexander became pale from his words.’ Note the classic poetic order with the prepositional ز placed before گفتار.
segments.s492-g144: روان here means ‘spirit/soul.’ پر ز indicates ‘full of’ (پر = full (of), ز = of/because of), درد = pain/sorrow, so روان پر ز درد = ‘his spirit full of pain.’ The following رخان لاژورد describes the face/cheeks: لاژورد = ‘lapis-lazuli (blue),’ and رخان (in this poetic usage) refers to the visage/cheeks’ state; together ‘and his (cheeks/face) were lapis-blue.’
segments.s492-g153: بدو گفت قیدافه is an inverted reporting clause: بدو (to him/unto him) is an indirect-object clitic phrase placed before the verb گفت (said), and the speaker's name قیدافه follows, i.e. 'Qeydafeh said to him'. The short clause کز داوری (کز = که + ز, 'from/through/in the act of') introduces the domain or reason of the admonition: 'in/when it comes to judgment'.
segments.s492-g154: لبت را بپرداز is an imperative instruction. لبت ('your lip' → here 'your speech/words') appears with the object marker را before the verb, a normal Persian word order for a definite object in an imperative. بپرداز is an archaic/poetic imperative (here 'turn away/keep back/cease') addressed to the second person. کاسکندری is a vocative/particle attached to the name (addressing Alexander); it follows the verb, giving the addressee.
segments.s492-g155: اگر (if) begins a conditional clause. چهره خویش is split into two written pieces (چهره 'face/appearance' + خوی + ش = خویش 'one's own'); the clitic ش is the possessive and attaches to خوی, so the phrase means 'your own face'. بینی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you see' (classical literary usage), and به چشم 'with/by the eye' specifies manner ('with your eye'/'to your sight').
segments.s492-g156: ز چاره بیاسای is an imperative or hortatory phrase: ز ('from/of') + چاره ('remedy/means') with بیاسای (an archaic second-person imperative 'be at ease/rest') telling the addressee to cease striving for a remedy. و links to the next injunction; منمای is negative imperative formed with the negative prefix م- + نما (from نشان دادن 'to show'), meaning 'do not show/reveal', and خشم is its object 'anger'.
segments.s492-g157: بیاورد و بنهاد are two past-tense narrative verbs ('he brought' and 'he placed/put down') joined by و. پیشش ('before him/in his presence') is a locative clitic phrase placed after the verbs, and حریر is the noun 'silk' describing the material of what was brought/placed. The sequence follows simple narrative word order: verb(s) then locative phrase and noun.
segments.s492-g158: نوشته here is a noun 'a writing/text' that specifies what was on the silk. The following برو is an archaic/localized prepositional glue (classical contraction of بر + او / 'on it' or similar) used to link the writing to the descriptive phrase; in modern terms it functions like 'on'/'in'. صورت دلپذیر is a noun phrase 'a pleasing/pleasant form/appearance' modifying how the writing is presented ('written in a pleasing form').
segments.s492-g159: که‌گر is a fused conditional particle (که گر ≈ اگر 'if, if indeed') introducing a counterfactual/contingent clause. هیچ جنبش بدی means 'any movement/stirring of badness'—هیچ with a following noun makes the clause indefinite/negative in flavor; در نگار 'in the picture/painting' locates the supposed motion. The clause sets up a hypothetical: 'if there had been any stirring in the picture...'.
segments.s492-g160: نبودی is an archaic past-form of 'to be' used here in the negative contingency (literary past: 'there would not have been / was not'); جز ('except') introduces the exclusive remainder, and اسکندر شهریار ('Alexander the king') is the exception. The whole short clause means 'there would have been none except Alexander the king.'
segments.s492-g129: بدو is the prepositional/adverbial pronoun 'to him' (directed toward that person). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' placed immediately after the indirect object (poetic/inverted word order). قیدافه‌ای بیطقون is the name/epithet of the speaker (قیدافه with the -ای ending giving a vocative/indefinite flavour, and بیطقون as an attached epithet/proper name); together they identify who spoke.
segments.s492-g130: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. خیره ماندی is a two-part predicate where خیره (gazing/fixed) is the predicative adjective/participle and ماندی is the past second-person singular 'you remained/stood' — together 'you stood staring'. به introduces the target and governs the noun phrase جزع اندرون ('the lamentation/inner distress'), so the full phrase asks why the addressee is standing staring at the inner lament.
segments.s492-g131: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. که introduces the subordinate clause. چونین means 'such'/'thus'. نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن ('is not' / 'would not be'); in this prophetic/poetic statement it conveys 'does not occur' or 'would not be'. به روم is a locative phrase 'in/at Rum (Byzantium)' with به used poetically to mark the place.
segments.s492-g132: که again links the following explanatory clause. آسیمه is an adjective 'distraught/aghast'; گشتی is the past second-person singular of گشتن 'you became/went' (here 'you became distraught') — the verb is used poetically as 'became'. بدین مایه is an adverbial phrase 'to this degree/extent' (بد- prefix + دین/این 'this' + مایه 'degree/amount'), and بوم is an archaic/cadential verbal/copu laic element in this idiom completing the sense 'come to / be in this condition' (here functioning idiomatically rather than as the common noun 'homeland').
segments.s492-g133: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. بدو is the indirect-object pronoun 'to him'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here listed senses do not include the verbal sense). کای is a vocative particle 'O' used to address someone directly, and شهریار is the vocative noun 'sovereign/king'. The order is subject – indirect object – verb – vocative, a poetic inversion.
segments.s492-g134: تو is the pronoun 'you' (subject). این خانه را is the direct object 'this house' with را marking definiteness/object. خوارمایه is an adjective/noun meaning 'despicable/lowly' applied to the house. مدار here is an imperative/negative admonition 'do not make/consider' (poetic command form), so the full clause is 'You, O king, do not make this house lowly.'
segments.s492-g135: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. ایوان شاهان is a genitive construction 'the porticoes of kings'. سرش is 'its head/top' with the enclitic ـش 'its'. برترست is the predicate 'is loftier/superior' (برتر + است fused), so the phrase states that its top surpasses the porticoes of other kings.
segments.s492-g136: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. ایوان تو is the subject 'your portico'. معدن is used metaphorically as 'a mine/source' and گوهرست is a predicate formed from گوهر + است ('is a gem/contains jewels') — together 'your portico is a mine of jewels.' The verb 'است' is incorporated into گوهرست in this poetic form.
segments.s492-g105: بدو گفت is literally 'to him said' — بدو is a dative prepositional phrase 'to him' placed before گفت ('he said') for 'he said to him'. کاکنون ('now') is an adverbial placed before the verb phrase. ره خانه گیر is an imperative command: ره ('road/way') + خانه ('home') as the object, and گیر is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'take/seize' — so 'take the road home.'
segments.s492-g106: بیاسای is an imperative 'be at ease / rest' addressed to the same addressee. با مردم دلپذیر is a prepositional phrase 'with pleasant people' (با + مردم) where دلپذیر postmodifies مردم ('pleasant/agreeable'), so the whole clause means 'rest among pleasant people.'
segments.s492-g107: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. فردا sets the time 'tomorrow'. بیایی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive form meaning 'you come' (poetic clause after چو). تو appears after the subordinate clause as an emphatic/indirect-object pronoun ('to you' or topical 'you'), and پاسخ دهم is a 1st-person verb phrase 'I will give an answer' — دهم is 1sg present/subjunctive/used futurally, so the line reads 'When you come tomorrow, I will answer (you).'
segments.s492-g108: به بر گشتنت is a prepositional temporal phrase 'upon/at your returning' (به + برگشتنت, where گشتن+ـت forms the verbal noun 'your return'). رای فرخ نهم is 'I will give a fortunate/favorable decision' — رای ('opinion/plan/decision') is the object and نهم is 1st-person singular 'I put/give' (used to mean 'I will give'). Word order places the temporal phrase and object before the verb in typical Persian/poetic inversion.
segments.s492-g109: سکندر is the subject ('Alexander') and بیامد is the simple past 'came'. سوی is the preposition 'toward/to'. خان خویش is a possessive phrase: خان (here a dwelling/hostel/court in context) followed by خویش ('his own'), which is formed from خوی + the enclitic ش; together it means 'his khan / his lodging / his court.'
segments.s492-g110: همه شب is an adverbial 'the whole night'. همی + ساخت gives a continuous past sense ('kept making / was making'): همی marks iterative/continuous aspect and ساخت is past 'made/prepared'. درمان خویش is the object 'his cure/remedy' (درمان + possessive خویش), so the clause means 'he spent the whole night making/preparing his remedy.'
segments.s492-g111: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بر زد is a compound verbal idea where زد ('struck') with the preverb بر conveys 'rose up / lifted' in poetic usage. سر ('head/top') is the thing that 'rose' and از کوه 'from the mountain' marks the origin. روشن چراغ ('bright lamp') names the thing rising — poetic word order places modifiers and nouns in a flexible order.
segments.s492-g112: Another چو 'when' clause: دیبا فروزنده is a nominal phrase 'radiant brocade' (دیبا = brocade, فروزنده = shining/radiant). شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became'. دشت و راغ ('the plain and the rāgh') are the subjects affected by that becoming, placed after the predicate by poetic inversion; overall: 'when the plain and the rāgh were made like shining brocade.'
segments.s492-g161: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'; the clause order is Subject + چو + verb + object/phrase: 'سکندر چو دید آن' = 'when Alexander saw that'. بخایید is an older/poetic intransitive verb meaning 'to gape/be astonished' and governs the following noun لب ('lip'); the phrase 'بخایید لب' = 'his lip(s) gaped' (the subject is Alexander, understood from context).
segments.s492-g162: This is the main clause: 'برو' functions adverbially (an immediate/abrupt marker here, from the verb 'go') followed by 'تیره شد روز' = 'the day became dark' (شد = become). 'چون تیره شب' is a simile 'as dark as night' (چون = 'like/as'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: adjective + شد + noun.
segments.s492-g163: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین = thus/so; گفت used as the verb 'said' in poetic diction). The phrase 'بی‌خنجری در نهان' is an adverbial/adjunct describing manner: بی‌خنجری = 'without a dagger, unarmed' and در نهان = 'in secret/secretly' (در + نهان).
segments.s492-g164: مبادا is a fixed optative/negative formula meaning 'may it not be' / 'lest'; it is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که + subjunctive/optative باشد ('that there be'). 'کس اندر جهان' = 'anyone in the world' is the logical subject of باشد. The construction expresses a wish/forbidding: 'May there be no one in the world...'.
segments.s492-g165: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him' (indirect object); گفت is the verb 'said' (he said to him). قیدافه‌گر is an epithet/vocative applied to the addressee (an older/compound epithet — here rendered roughly 'quibbler' or 'one who adds cavils'). خنجرت is 'khunjar' with the 2nd-person possessive suffix -ت = 'your dagger' (object being addressed).
segments.s492-g166: This is an imperative/request sequence: حمایل here names the harness/scabbard/support (used as the thing to be put on), followed by بدی which in the verse-form appears to function as an imperative/verb 'put on/gird' (this form is ambiguous in the provided sense list — see note). 'پیش من' = 'before me' (location), and 'بر برت' = 'upon you' (بر + برت, the second element having the 2nd-person suffix). Grammatically it's a series of imperatives/commands directed at the addressee.
segments.s492-g167: The pair with repeated نه marks a correlative negative 'neither... nor'. 'نیروت' is نیروی + -ت (your strength) meaning 'your strength/force'; بودی is past-tense 'were/was' (here elliptical: 'you had/there was'). 'نه شمشیر تیز' continues the negation: 'nor a sharp sword' (no copula is needed in Persian noun phrases).
segments.s492-g168: Continuation of the negation: 'نه جای نبرد' = 'no place of battle' (جای = place, نبرد = battle), 'و نه راه گریز' = 'and no way of escape' (راه = way/path, گریز = escape). The verbs are omitted by ellipsis after the first clause (typical parallelism).
segments.s492-g25: This is an exclamatory/subordinate clause introduced by که with چه as the interrogative/exclamative 'what' (literal: 'what came'). بر + شهر فریان marks the location 'upon the city of Faryan'. آمد is a simple past (3rd sg.) used impersonally/happeningly ('befell'/'happened to'). ز introduces the cause 'from/because of', followed by رنج 'suffering'. Word order is topical location → question word → verb → cause.
segments.s492-g26: نماند is the past tense (here with negative sense) meaning 'there remained' / 'was left' (context implies 'there remained none'). The list that follows (افسر و تخت و لشکر نه گنج) are the items that did not remain; نه before گنج functions as 'nor' (classical style). The verb precedes the enumerated nouns, a typical poetic order to emphasize loss.
segments.s492-g27: مرا here functions as the indirect object 'to me' (written in the line split as م + را); را is the definite/object marker. این‌که ... آمد همی با عروس is a subordinate clause 'that he came with the bride' (آمد is past; همی is a frequentive/poetic particle emphasizing ongoing or simple past action, often left untranslated). با marks accompaniment 'with'. The clause attaches to the following main verb رها کرد ('he freed/gave').
segments.s492-g28: رها کرد is a compound of the verbal noun رها ('release/free') plus کرد 'he did/made', giving a past transitive 'he released/freed (someone)'. The object (the bride) is elided here but picked up from the previous segment; ز introduces the source/agent 'from Alexander Filqus' (poetic use of ز = 'from'). The two proper names follow z in normal order.
segments.s492-g29: وگرنه = 'otherwise'. بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause here (so that/that), and گردنم is the object 'my neck' (گردن + ـم). The full subordinate clause continues with زنند in the next segment.
segments.s492-g30: زن + ند is verb + 3rd-plural ending (زنند = 'they strike/they cut'); the tokenizer split the stem and ending. The conjunction و links the second commanded result. به آتش is a prepositional phrase 'into/in the fire' (به used adverbially/locative), بسوزد is subjunctive/optative 'may be burned' (reported command), and تنم is 'my body' (تن + ـم) as the patient. The segment thus records two ordered punishments.
segments.s492-g31: کنون 'now' sets the time. هرچ 'whatever' (or 'whatever it is') + باید 'must' form a nominal content 'whatever must be done'. به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well' (به used here to form an adverbial, خوبی 'goodness' = 'well'), and بکن is the imperative 'do (it)'. The clause is an instruction: 'Now do whatever must be done well.'
segments.s492-g32: برو is the imperative 'go'. هیـچ here means 'nothing/anything' in a negative command context and scopes with the verbs that follow. مشکن is a prohibitive form 'do not break' (negative imperative). بخواهش is 'ask of him' (بخواه + ـش = 'ask him'), with سخن 'words/speech' as the object: 'ask him for no words' (i.e., ask for nothing/say nothing). The sequence gives three short orders: go, break nothing, and ask him for no speech.
segments.s492-g97: This clause lists two nouns (خردمندی و شرم) as a compound nominal phrase followed by the predicative adjective نزدیک. The final تست is an archaic/petrified copula ('thou art' / 'is') that links the predicate to the (understood) second‑person subject; word order is Topic (compound noun) + Predicative adjective + copula. In modern Persian one would say نزدیکِ توست or تو را نزدیک است.
segments.s492-g98: Here جهان is the topical subject and ایمن is the predicative adjective; the prepositional phrase از رای باریک ('from (the) subtle counsel') specifies what the world is protected from. The clause closes again with the archaic copula تست. Word order: subject + predicate + 'from' phrase + copula.
segments.s492-g99: This is a conditional/time clause. کنون‌گر functions like 'کنون گر' = 'if now' (an archaic conditional marker + temporal), followed by a negative verb form نتابی (negative prefix ن- + تابِی, here meaning 'turn'/'move'). The phrase سر از X (سر از باژ و ساو) is an idiom 'turn the head from / take one's head away from' (i.e. turn away). باژ و ساو are two coordinated nouns (likely names/places).
segments.s492-g100: بدانی is a jussive/imperative/subjunctive 2nd‑person form meaning 'know' (introducing the content clause). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. با ما is a prepositional phrase 'with us'; نداری is a present negative predicate meaning 'you do not have' (literally 'lack'); تو is the explicit 2nd‑person subject/emphatic pronoun and تاو (here a noun) is the thing lacking ('radiance/glow').
segments.s492-g101: نبینی is a negative verb form ('you do not see' / 'you see not') with the subject omitted (you). The fixed expression به جز (= 'except') introduces the exception phrase خوبی و راستی ('goodness and uprightness'), which is the only thing seen. Word order: negative verb + prepositional/exclusive phrase + objects.
segments.s492-g102: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. پیچی is the second‑person verb 'you turn' (present/imperative sense) and is followed by the idiom سر از + (X) = 'turn one's head away from X'. The coordinated nouns کژی و کاستی are the things turned away from. Overall: 'When you turn (your) head from crookedness and deficiency...'.
segments.s492-g103: برآشفت is a past tense verbal form meaning 'became upset/angry' (3rd person), with the prefix بر- intensifying the state. قیدافه is the subject (a proper name). چو این شنید is a temporal subordinate clause ('when / upon which he heard this') where چون introduces the temporal ('when/after') relation and شنید is the past tense 'he heard'.
segments.s492-g104: بجز خامشی is a short phrase 'except silence' (بجز = except; خامشی = silence). چاره آن is the noun phrase 'a remedy for that' (چاره = remedy; آن = that/it), and ندید is the past tense 'he did not see' (i.e. 'he saw no solution'). Together: 'he saw no remedy for it except silence.'
